title: Cat Stopped Using Scratching Post: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your cat stopped using their scratching post. Learn when to see a vet and how photos help diagnose potential health or behavior issues.
content_html:
What Does This Behavior Change Mean?

When a cat stops using their scratching post, it is rarely a simple act of defiance. Scratching is an essential feline behavior used for territorial marking, stretching, and shedding the outer layers of their claws. A complete cessation of this activity often suggests that the physical act of scratching has become painful, or that a change in their environment has made them feel less secure in their space.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for a cat ignoring their scratching post is Low. This is not typically a life-threatening situation; however, it is a behavioral shift that should be addressed during a scheduled veterinary visit. While you do not need to seek emergency care, you should monitor your cat for other subtle signs of illness, such as changes in mobility, hiding more than usual, or sensitivity when their paws are touched.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Sharing a photo of your cat’s paws and their scratching post can provide vital clues for a veterinary professional. High-quality images of the claws can help identify if they are overgrown, split, or showing signs of a nail bed infection. Additionally, a photo of the scratching post itself allows a vet or behaviorist to assess the material and stability; if a post is wobbly or the carpet is too worn, a cat may stop using it simply because it no longer feels safe or effective.

faqs.question: Could my cat have arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially in older cats. Osteoarthritis can make the vertical stretching and pulling required for scratching very painful, leading them to abandon the post.

faqs.question: Should I try a different type of scratching surface?
faqs.answer: It is a good idea. Some cats develop a preference for horizontal scratching pads over vertical posts as they age or if they experience joint discomfort.

faqs.question: Can overgrown claws cause this?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. If a cat's claws are too long, they can get painfully snagged in the fibers of the post, which may cause the cat to develop an aversion to using it.
